title: Born in Newton, Baker County, 1930
summary: Will Griffin opens the March 9, 2013 interview at Albany State University, recorded with videographer John Bishop for the Civil Rights History Project, introducing Louise Broadway of the Baker County Movement. She gives her full name, says she was born in 1930 in the small town of Newton in Baker County, Georgia, and recalls a childhood as the child of farming parents who owned no farm.

title: Walking to a Cold School as Buses Passed
summary: Broadway describes walking to school every morning, sometimes ten or fifteen miles, hands too cold to feel until they warmed around a potbelly stove. She recalls that the white children rode school buses that passed the Black children walking the same roads in the same neighborhoods, and that she knew even then it was unfair.

title: Seven Grades in One Church Sanctuary
summary: She explains that school was held inside a church with no partitions, one teacher seating the first through seventh grades by section across three pews and teaching each in turn while the others kept quiet. She names two of her teachers, Miss Salome and Miss Mary Hawkins, and recalls graduating from the seventh grade and going on to high school in Newton.

title: Two Colored High Schools and a House Full
summary: Broadway recalls that Newton had two colored high schools, east and west, and that she attended Newton Colored High School, where classes met in army barracks brought in by the state. As the youngest of four, with one brother and two sisters, she describes a doting older sister and a family of six living in a one-room country shack with a single fireplace.

title: Church First, and a Dollar a Day
summary: She recalls her parents teaching her to make do with what they had and to put church before any school activity, her father working at a grist mill grinding corn into meal and her mother working as a maid. After graduating in 1948 she wanted college but her sharecropper father and maid mother earned roughly fifty cents to a dollar a day, so her first job was as a maid for a dollar a day for a family she names as Mamie White.

title: Marrying Grady Broadway in 1949
summary: Broadway recounts meeting Grady Broadway Junior, who was raised by an older brother after their parents died young, through visits to a cousin and at church where the two would only smile at one another. When he later moved into her community they reconnected as old friends, married in 1949, and went on to have five children together.

title: Knowing It Was Unfair
summary: Asked when she first noticed racial conditions, Broadway points back to childhood, to being unable to ride the bus and to white children throwing spitballs at Black children walking to school. She says she always knew it was unfair, but felt there was nothing she could do until the Civil Rights Act was passed and the segregated schools came up for change.

title: Enrolling a Son in the White School
summary: In the 1950s, while her husband sharecropped for a local white farmer, the Broadways enrolled their eldest child, Jesse Lee Broadway, in the all-white school. When the farmer heard, he told her husband to withdraw the boy or settle his entire debt before year's end, so they withdrew him until the year ended, after which her husband stopped working for that farmer and they were able to send three children to the school the next year.

title: A Son Hospitalized, Racing Home to Protect Them
summary: Broadway describes the widespread intimidation, including one son beaten so badly at school that he was hospitalized for days, and white youths following Black children off the bus to attack them. Working in Albany, she got a call that her children were under attack and drove home so fast the speedometer chain broke, and recalls that no one was punished though the school did begin patrolling its grounds.

part: Bloody Saturday and the Baker County Movement
title: Treating the Beaten on Bloody Saturday
summary: Broadway worked thirty years as assistant to Dr. J. L. Shirley, a medical doctor in Albany, who treated for free the civil rights workers and citizens beaten by a mob on what she calls Bloody Saturday after the nearby town of Camilla refused them care. She recalls Sherrod being brought in bruised, and that her own St. Matthew's Missionary Baptist Church was fired upon as the marchers regrouped and lay down among the pews.
related_events: Bloody Saturday march in Baker County

title: Secretary of the Baker County Movement
summary: As one of the secretaries of the Baker County Movement, Broadway took notes at the meetings as citizens testified to mistreatment, such as a child struck in the head with a stick, and says some of those notes now rest at the East Baker Historical Society in Newton. She frames the demand not as integration for its own sake but as equal opportunity, recalling that Black schools received the white schools' worn and torn books a year later.

title: Dr. Shirley's Gift to the Movement
summary: Broadway affirms that Dr. J. L. Shirley supported the movement, willingly treating the injured of Bloody Saturday rather than turning them away as the doctors in Camilla had. She notes that he knew she was involved in the movement.

title: First Black Worker on the County Ambulance
summary: Broadway recounts that the county commissioners offered a free emergency-care course, and that after her workdays at Dr. Shirley's office she trained at night to become, by her account, the first Black person in Baker County to complete it and ride the ambulance. She worked the ambulance without pay at all hours, motivated by a wish for people of her own color to be helped by someone of their color.

title: The Bloodiest Day and the March to Register
summary: Asked for anything more about her movement work, Broadway returns to the bloodiest day she witnessed, when a woman shielded Sherrod and may have saved his life as people were beaten to the ground. She recalls marching from a local church to the courthouse to register people to vote, with Sherrod among them.
related_events: Voter registration march in Baker County

title: Knocked Down, Then Back the Next Morning
summary: Broadway describes a county official confronting Sherrod as an outsider with no right to be there and knocking him into a corner before ordering him to leave, after which the marchers withdrew to the church. She stresses they returned the very next morning and kept on until things improved, and recalls that when she finally registered during this time she was allowed simply to sign her name without a literacy test.
related_events: Voter registration march in Baker County

title: What Became of the Injured, and a Cruel Man
summary: Asked what happened to those beaten and injured, Broadway says they recovered and stayed with the movement until people were registered to vote, and that conditions in Baker County gradually calmed. She describes a local white man who harassed Black travelers, stopped them for tickets, ran a service station with whites-only signs, and was eventually felled by a stroke years before his death.

title: Freedom of Speech and a Doctor's Bills Forgiven
summary: Broadway reflects that the movement won Black residents the right to speak freely where before a word to a white person could bring a beating, and that desegregating the schools gave people courage and wisdom to stand up. Returning to Dr. Shirley, she confirms he never charged the students and citizens beaten on Bloody Saturday, calling the free care his contribution to the movement.

title: Closing Thanks
summary: The interviewer thanks Louise Broadway for sharing her story, and the recording closes as a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
